body_txt: Sometimes being immortal or a member of a really Long-Lived race means that aging occurs naturally or rapidly until a character physically reaches their mid-twenties. At this point, their aging stops, and they get to be young and beautiful forever, or at least for a very long time. This is generally based on a distinction being made between aging and growing . Growing is the process of a body "building" itself to adulthood according to more or less consistent inborn instructions, and as such is a natural biological process that's "intended" to occur. It is more or less complete between the end of puberty and the early twenties. Aging is instead the gradual wearing down and decay of the body as a lifetime of wear and tear, injuries, and simple entropy slowly catch up with it and cause its systems to become worn out and to break down. In cases where this trope is at play, what often occurs is that an immortal's body becomes so efficient at reparing itself and keeping itself in good condition that aging ceases to occur or at least does so at an imperceptible pace, while growth and puberty still occur over whatever timeframe they normally would. This is usually the kind of immortality associated with having a strong Healing Factor , as part of its super-efficient repair of all damage to the body and its tissues and organs. In other instances, immortality might instead "freeze" the body at whatever stage it was when it is acquired, in which case some people might end up with eternally teenaged or early adult bodies, alongside others with perpetually elderly or childish appearances. If immortality is a form of power that kicks in during puberty , this might skew the overall nature of immortals' bodies. If instead a person's mind or soul ends up supernaturally tethered to their body without necessarily changing how that body operates or heals itself, you end up with Age Without Youth ... or with just plain undeath , which might be considered a more extreme form of the former. This is a common feature for elves , even if they aren't technically immortal but are just long-lived. A character that's Younger Than They Look may fall under this if they weren't artificially created ; sometimes a character looks like a teen or young adult but is only chronologically in the single digits of age, maybe even Born as an Adult . With live-action productions, this is often used because there is an unavoidable upper limit to how long a child actor can play a given role before noticeably aging, but a good makeup department can cover up evidence of aging in an adult actor for years. Contrast Proportional Aging , Age Without Youth , and Not Growing Up Sucks . noreallife
